narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school and then hearing your application won't move forward because our model sees a 54% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam feels deeply unfair, especially with your strong 3.8 undergraduate GPA shining through. But let me walk you through the story this model tells about your path, like piecing together chapters from your academic journey. The biggest chapter, the one that weighed heaviest, was your Year 3 decile of 1.0—landing at the bottom of your class that year signaled to the model a tough climb in the rigorous demands of law school, pulling strongly toward the risk of failure. Right behind it was your Year 1 decile, also a 1.0, painting a picture of early struggles that didn't build the momentum others might have. Your LSAT score of 22 then stepped in as the next big influence, suggesting the analytical speed and precision law exams demand could be a hurdle. Layered on top was your lower-middle family income, which the model associates with added pressures that sometimes tip the scales against bar success. Your Native American background, while a source of resilience and unique perspective, unfortunately correlated with higher risks in the patterns the model has seen. Even your solid UGPA took a backseat here, nudged toward failure by how it fit into this overall narrative, alongside being female and planning full-time studies—subtler threads that together wove a cautious tale. It's not that any one part defines you, but their combination whispered "higher odds of not passing" to the model, leading to this tough call. You've got strengths that stand out, like that impressive GPA, and this isn't the end of your story—perhaps targeting schools with stronger support for students like you or bolstering those deciles could rewrite the next chapter. Hang in there; your determination will carry you far.
